segments.title: Inconstant's unarmoured iron hull and unusually long afterlife
segments.summary: Meet the iron-hulled screw frigate that entered service without armour, later became a training ship, and survived until the 1950s.
segments.body: Inconstant combined an iron hull with no armour, placing her at an important transitional point between wooden sailing frigates and later protected cruisers. Her later names and training roles kept the hull in service for decades, but she was ultimately scrapped before large-scale warship preservation had become established.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the unarmoured iron-hulled frigate and traces her training-ship afterlife to scrapping.

segments.title: The Wampanoag threat behind Inconstant's design
segments.summary: See why Edward Reed planned six fast frigates against an American design threat, then stopped after three when that threat faded.
segments.body: The Royal Navy conceived Inconstant as the first of six fast unarmoured frigates responding to the American Wampanoag class. Once the American ships proved flawed, the strategic need weakened and the expense of the British design helped cut the programme to three vessels.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Reed's six-ship plan, its American stimulus, and why only three were built.

segments.title: Eleven boilers, a giant propeller, and 16 knots
segments.summary: Explore the machinery and dimensions that made Inconstant a fast, long-legged steam-and-sail frigate for her era.
segments.body: Eleven boilers fed a single trunk engine driving a 23-foot propeller, producing 7,360 indicated horsepower and a claimed 16-knot top speed. The discussion also distinguishes her coal-limited steaming range from the much less constrained endurance available under her full ship rig.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the ship's machinery, dimensions, horsepower, speed, rig, and stated steaming range.

segments.title: Inconstant's 9-inch and 7-inch rifled muzzle-loaders
segments.summary: Compare the ammunition and useful fighting ranges of Inconstant's heavy rifled muzzle-loading broadside guns.
segments.body: Inconstant carried ten 9-inch and six 7-inch rifled muzzle-loaders, with Palliser and other shell types available for the smaller weapons. Dr. Clarke stresses the practical difference between advertised maximum range and effective range, placing the useful reach at roughly 9,000 yards for the 9-inch gun and 5,000 for the 7-inch.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the gun battery, ammunition, surviving examples, and effective ranges.

segments.title: Armour-piercing guns on an unarmoured ship
segments.summary: The ship's broadside could threaten iron armour even though Inconstant herself carried no armour protection.
segments.body: The main-deck 9-inch guns were credited with penetrating 11.3 inches of wrought iron, while the upper-deck 7-inch weapons could pierce 7.7 inches. This creates the defining tactical contradiction of the design: powerful armour-piercing firepower mounted in a hull that could not absorb comparable punishment.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the guns' stated wrought-iron penetration with the ship's lack of armour.

segments.title: Channel Squadron service followed by eight years in reserve
segments.summary: Follow Inconstant from Pembroke construction and a prestigious Channel posting into the Victorian reserve system.
segments.body: After fitting out at Portsmouth, Inconstant commissioned for the Channel Squadron and later joined Frederick Seymour's Detached Squadron. Her subsequent eight years laid up illustrate how the Victorian Royal Navy preserved hulls and reduced peacetime operating costs while retaining the ability to expand in war.

evidence.note: The transcript covers construction, commissioning, early squadron duty, payoff, and the logic of reserve service.

segments.title: A flagship for Seymour and Victorian gunboat diplomacy
segments.summary: See why a senior admiral recalled Inconstant from reserve and used her to lead a globe-circling Detached Squadron.
segments.body: Frederick Seymour's decision to request Inconstant as a Mediterranean Fleet flagship is presented as evidence that experienced officers valued the ship. Her later Detached Squadron assignment joined global cruising to coercive naval diplomacy, using a visible concentration of firepower to reinforce British demands abroad.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Seymour's choice and the Detached Squadron's diplomatic purpose and world cruise.

segments.title: World cruise, shipboard fire, and the Anglo-Egyptian War
segments.summary: Trace Inconstant's eventful circumnavigation through a royal passenger, a fire at sea, and operations in Egypt.
segments.body: The cruise included the future George V, a diversion toward South Africa during the First Boer War, and a return-voyage fire controlled by flooding after compartments. The squadron then diverted to Egypt, where Inconstant participated in the Anglo-Egyptian War and landed sailors for operations ashore.

evidence.note: The transcript recounts the voyage, fire response, Egyptian diversion, bombardment context, and landing party.

segments.title: From fighting ship to accommodation and training hulk
segments.summary: Follow the old frigate's later transformation into accommodation, gunnery, boys', and torpedo-training roles.
segments.body: After returning to reserve, Inconstant became accommodation for Devonport barracks overflow and then served successive training establishments under new names. Her long reuse shows how a hull could remain institutionally valuable long after it ceased to be a credible front-line cruiser.

evidence.note: The transcript traces reserve, accommodation, gunnery, boys' training, torpedo training, and final scrapping.

segments.title: A fast sailing frigate with retractable steam machinery
segments.summary: Learn how Inconstant reduced drag under sail and protected her iron hull for long ocean deployments.
segments.body: Inconstant reportedly reached 13.5 knots under sail, aided by a hoistable propeller and funnels that could be lowered to reduce drag. Two layers of oak separated her iron hull from copper sheathing, limiting fouling while also contributing to her qualities as a seaboat and gun platform.

evidence.note: The transcript describes sailing speed, the hoistable propeller, lowered funnels, oak backing, and copper covering.

segments.title: Why Inconstant made sense on distant ocean stations
segments.summary: The ship's speed and ocean mobility were useful abroad, where she was less likely to meet newer protected or armoured cruisers.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats Inconstant's repeated flagship service as practical proof of her usefulness rather than merely paper performance. Sending an unarmoured cruiser into open oceans and distant stations exploited her manoeuvrability while reducing the chance that she would face the protected and armoured opponents she could not safely fight.

evidence.note: The transcript links Seymour's confidence and Detached Squadron deployment to the ship's strengths and protection limits.

segments.title: Comparing Inconstant, Raleigh, and Shah
segments.summary: Compare the three related frigates by dockyard, machinery supplier, construction date, cost, and armament.
segments.body: The class comparison shows that Inconstant, Raleigh, and Shah came from different Royal Dockyards and machinery firms, with years separating their construction. Their costs also varied substantially, while Shah carried a revised battery of two 9-inch, sixteen 7-inch, and eight 5-inch guns.

evidence.note: The transcript compares the three ships' builders, engines, dates, costs, and Shah's armament.

segments.title: HMS Shah at the Battle of Pacocha
segments.summary: Watch two unarmoured British ships struggle to contain the turret ship Huáscar in shallow water and launch the first torpedo fired in anger.
segments.body: Shah and the corvette Amethyst had to respect Huáscar's turret guns, while the smaller Peruvian ship used the shallow water to manoeuvre more freely. Shah fired the first self-propelled torpedo used in combat, but it missed, and the action exposed the tactical limits of speed and firepower without protection.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Battle of Pacocha, Huáscar's shallow-water advantage, and Shah's missed torpedo.

segments.title: Shah's soldiers, naval guns ashore, and Bermuda afterlife
segments.summary: Follow Shah from troop transport and naval-brigade support in southern Africa to reserve and accommodation service in Bermuda.
segments.body: On her homeward voyage Shah diverted to carry troops to Durban and contributed sailors and heavy naval guns to the Anglo-Zulu War. After a short return to Britain and reserve status, she moved to Bermuda for accommodation duty and eventually became a coal-storage hulk.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Shah's South African diversion, naval contingent, reserve status, Bermuda accommodation role, and coal hulk conversion.

segments.title: How pieces of Shah survived after the ship
segments.summary: Shah disappeared as a hull, but her iron masts, stern plaque, and crew monument preserved parts of her story.
segments.body: Shah's iron masts were judged a lighter and more durable replacement for Victory's wooden masts and remain associated with the preserved flagship. Her stern plaque survives in Bermuda, while a Portsmouth monument commemorates her crew, spreading the ship's material legacy across several sites.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the surviving masts, plaque, and crew monument.

segments.title: The industrial dilemma behind building a minor navy
segments.summary: A closing design exercise asks whether a smaller South American state should buy ships abroad or first build the industry needed to sustain a navy.
segments.body: The unanswered exercise places the viewer in charge of a smaller South American state in the 1870s or 1880s. It frames naval power as an industrial question as well as a ship-buying decision: domestic construction demands iron, suppliers, skilled labour, and infrastructure that may be harder to acquire than the warships themselves.

evidence.note: The transcript poses an audience exercise about naval force design and industrial development for a minor South American state.
